Client 1: Yep.
Advisor: David, what are you using as your household gross income for the year—ballpark is fine, but I’ll grab the exact number you’re tracking.
Client 1: The number we’ve been using is 135450 for annual gross household income.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And Susan, does that line up with what you think of as the household income, or do you think of it differently?
Client 2: Yeah, that’s the figure we’ve been using—135450. I usually just think “around one-thirty-five,” but that’s the exact one.

Client 2: It feels like a lot. Around ten-ish? I don’t know—groceries, utilities, kids stuff, everything.
Advisor: Totally fair. When you say “ten-ish,” do you mean about ten thousand a month all-in? If you have the exact monthly total you track, even better.
Client 1: Yeah—our total monthly expenses number is 10400 per month.
Advisor: Okay, 10400 monthly. And within that, what are the monthly debt payments—mainly the mortgage—what’s the monthly payment amount?
Client 2: The mortgage is… I always say forty-three hundred. That’s the payment we feel every month.

Advisor: Okay, 10400 monthly. And within that, what are the monthly debt payments—mainly the mortgage—what’s the monthly payment amount?
Client 2: The mortgage is… I always say forty-three hundred. That’s the payment we feel every month.
Advisor: Great—so monthly debt payments total are 4300, just to be precise?
Client 1: Yes, 4300 total monthly debt payments—just the mortgage for us.
Advisor: Thanks. One sec—just noting that down. Now, let’s shift to what you’ve built up on the investment side. When you think “investable assets,” what’s the total you’re working with across the accounts?

Client 1: Yes, 4300 total monthly debt payments—just the mortgage for us.
Advisor: Thanks. One sec—just noting that down. Now, let’s shift to what you’ve built up on the investment side. When you think “investable assets,” what’s the total you’re working with across the accounts?
Client 1: Total investable assets is 693550.
Advisor: Okay. And just so I’m not missing anything, that includes the joint taxable account, your retirement accounts, Susan’s bank-type account, and the private markets piece—right?
Client 2: Yes, that’s all the investable stuff. The house is separate in my head.

Client 2: Yes, that’s all the investable stuff. The house is separate in my head.
Advisor: Exactly—home value is its own bucket. While we’re here, can you give me the quick rundown of what makes up that 693550, just high level?
Client 1: Sure. There’s a joint taxable account—411600—held on a retirement platform. Then my 401k/IRA bucket at a brokerage is 215600.
Client 2: And I’ve got a smaller bank-type account on a retirement platform—35800. And David has that private markets thing—30550—on an advisor platform.
Advisor: Great, thank you. And then the home—primary residence—what value are you using for that today?

Client 1: 399850 is the value we use for the house.
Advisor: Got it. Now liabilities: you mentioned it’s basically the mortgage. What’s the current balance outstanding?
Client 1: Mortgage balance is 200600.
Advisor: And do you know the interest rate on it?
Client 2: I think it’s five and a half-ish—

Client 1: Yep.
Advisor: David, what are you using as your household gross income for the year—ballpark is fine, but I’ll grab the exact number you’re tracking.
Client 1: The number we’ve been using is 135450 for annual gross household income.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And Susan, does that line up with what you think of as the household income, or do you think of it differently?
Client 2: Yeah, that’s the figure we’ve been using—135450. I usually just think “around one-thirty-five,” but that’s the exact one.

Advisor: Hi David, hi Susan—good to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we dive into numbers, what would make today’s meeting a win for you?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m David. I’d say a win is: confirm we’re not overextending with the mortgage and kids’ costs, and then get clear next steps—like what to do with the accounts.
Client 2: And I’m Susan. I want it to feel… realistic. Like, if something goes sideways health-wise or the market drops, we’re still okay.
Advisor: That makes sense. Let’s start with the cash-flow picture—income coming in, then expenses and the mortgage, and then we’ll map that to your investments and protection. Sound okay?
Client 1: Yep.

Client 2: Yes, US.
Advisor: Perfect. Last admin-ish one: for planning, what tax bracket band should I assume you’re in right now?
Client 1: We’re in the 32% bracket band.
Advisor: Great—thank you. And Susan, when you said two dependents, that’s the kids you mentioned earlier—no other dependents like a parent you’re supporting?
Client 2: Right, just the kids. No one else.

Advisor: First one—marital status. Are you two married?
Client 1: Yes, we’re married.
Advisor: Great. Next—how would you describe your overall risk tolerance? Like conservative, moderate, aggressive—what fits best?
Client 2: I’d say moderate. I’m a little cautious, but not, like, all-cash cautious.
Advisor: Okay, moderate—got it.

Client 2: Yes, as long as it stays understandable.
Client 1: Works for me.
Advisor: Perfect. Before we wrap, I want to make sure the debt picture is clean. Besides the mortgage, do either of you have any other debts—car loans, credit cards you carry, student loans—anything like that?
Client 2: No, nothing like that. We pay cards off, so non-mortgage debt outstanding is 0.
Advisor: Great—thank you, Susan. And just to anchor the big numbers again: what value are you using for the home right now?

Client 1: It’s listed as Other.
Advisor: Other—okay. I’ll leave it at that for now.
Advisor: Last admin-ish one: how did you come to work with us—what advisory channel should I note? For example, through an RIA, a bank, an insurance agent—what’s the right description?
Client 2: It’s RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Perfect, thank you.

Client 2: Thank you—yes, that’s what I need.
Advisor: Thanks, Susan. And David, I hear you on the cash-flow guardrails. Before next time, I just want to lock down a few household basics so I’m not assuming wrong things.

Client 1: Yes, we’re married.

Client 1: Executive—yeah, exec.
Advisor: Perfect. And for your income, I know we’ve got the household figure we’re using, but what’s your gross annual income on your side, David—before taxes and deductions?
Client 1: My gross annual income is 89600.
Advisor: Great—thank you. And Susan, just so I’m not assuming, you’re still retired, right?
Client 2: Yes, I’m retired.

Advisor: First, employment status—are you currently employed, retired, self-employed, unemployed… what’s accurate today?
Client 1: I’m employed.

Client 1: Executive—yeah, exec.
